transform_insights.name: Loop Interchange
transform_insights.description: Swaps the loop nesting order so that the inner loop iterates over the fastest-varying (contiguous) dimension of the matrix M. Before: outer=j (columns), inner=i (rows) caused column-major traversal. After: outer=i (rows), inner=j (columns) achieves row-major traversal matching C's memory layout.

why_helped.explanation: Row-major access (stride-1) means consecutive M[i][j] and M[i][j+1] are adjacent in memory and share the same cache line. Column-major access jumped by N*sizeof(double) bytes each iteration, missing L1 on nearly every access.
Sequential access enables hardware prefetcher to predict and fetch next cache lines. Column-major access created an irregular pattern the prefetcher could not follow.
5.5x speedup entirely from eliminating memory stalls. The CPU was spending >80% of time waiting for cache misses before interchange.
With data arriving from L1 instead of L2/L3/DRAM, the pipeline stays full. IPC jumps from memory-bound ~1.8 to compute-bound ~10.3.

trade_offs: Legality: Loop interchange is only legal when there are no loop-carried dependencies that would be violated by reversing the iteration order. LLVM's dependence analysis must prove this.
Profitability heuristic: Not all interchanges help — if both loops already access memory sequentially, interchange may hurt. LLVM's cost model checks the access patterns before transforming.
Instruction count unchanged: This transform changes only iteration order, not the computation. The same number of instructions execute, just in a cache-friendlier order.
